nodes.title: 5. Punctuation
nodes.summary: This document provides a comprehensive guide to punctuation, emphasizing its importance for clarity and correct meaning in writing. It details the proper usage of apostrophes for possessives, brackets for secondary information and abbreviations, colons for introducing lists, and commas for separating items, introductory phrases, and expansive information. The guide advises against exclamation marks and outlines the sparing use of footnotes. It also covers the correct application of full stops, hyphenation rules for compound words and adjectives, and the distinction between double and single quotation marks for various contexts. Finally, it explains the use of semicolons for intermediate pauses and square brackets for clarifying quoted material.
nodes.text:
## 5. Punctuation

Getting it right

Correct text is also a matter of attention to detail. A misplaced comma can give a sentence a whole new meaning. This chapter runs through the most important rules, listed in alphabetical order, to help you write your text as correctly as possible.

ABN·AMRO

Writing Guide • January 2026
Punctuation

### Apostrophes

The apostrophe is used to show the possessive case, as in: 'ABN AMRO's profits', 'two weeks' holiday', 'a year's data', 'the member's point of view'. Note that the apostrophe is not used for plurals, dates or abbreviations. Correct spelling: two photos, the 1990s, three MPs.

### Brackets

Use brackets sparingly. Preferably, start a new sentence if you wish to clarify, explain or extrapolate.

- Use brackets when defining abbreviations, such as the 'British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC)'.
- Brackets indicate that the words are of secondary importance. (If a whole sentence is within brackets, put the full stop inside.)
- Putting brackets around a word or part of a word cannot be used as an alternative for 'or' and 'and/or', like in Dutch. For example: 'ABN AMRO's (international) operations are flourishing' does not mean that ABN AMRO's national and international operations are flourishing but simply that ABN AMRO's international operations are flourishing.

### Colons

Colons are used mainly to introduce an example, a list or bullets. For example:

There are several options:

- a
- b
- c

There is no space before the colon.

### Commas

Use commas sparingly. Start a new sentence if at all possible. Use a comma in the following circumstances:

- To separate items in a list, as in: 'The CEO stressed collaboration, revenue growth and low costs.'
- To separate introductory words or a conditional phrase, as in: 'Unfortunately, we were unable to...' or 'If you can understand this, you have no problem at all in using the comma.'
- To separate a phrase that adds expansive information, as in: 'Revenues increased to EUR 327 million, or 44.7%, compared with the same period last year.'
- When quoting someone, place a comma inside the quotation marks, as in: "I would be happy to help you," Hannah said.

Example

Compare the following:

'The advisers, who did not comply with regulations, were reprimanded' (expansive, all advisers were reprimanded)

'The advisers who did not comply with regulations were reprimanded' (limited, only those advisers who did not comply with regulations were reprimanded. No commas here.)

Writing Guide • January 2026
Punctuation

### Exclamation marks

Avoid using these, as they feel exaggerated to the reader.

### Footnotes: asterisks or numbers

Use footnotes sparingly, as they make your text harder to follow. On top of that, readers tend to skip footnotes. Never provide new or key information in a footnote. Use a footnote to provide additional or background information on a passage in your text, but only if it is impossible to provide the explanation in the text itself. Refer to the footnote by using an asterisk (*). In the event of multiple footnotes, use numbers in superscript ('). Never use footnotes in online texts or emails.

### Full stops

Use plenty. They make sentences easy to read and understand. Only use one space after a full stop. Never use two full stops at the end of a sentence, even if the sentence ends in an abbreviation. Do not use full stops after 'Mr', 'Mrs', 'Miss' or 'Ms' in British English, at the end of headings or at the end of hyperlinks.

### Hyphenation

English compound nouns are usually written as separate words and are rarely hyphenated. Occasionally, a hyphen is used in the following cases:

- Compounds with consecutive vowels: 'anti-inflationary'
- Compounds with certain prefixes: 'non-native', 'ex-partner'
- A prefix followed by a capitalised noun or adjective: 'anti-American'
- If a number, a separate letter or a symbol is part of the composite word: '4-way switch'.
- Compound adjectives preceding a noun: 'a well-read book'. Do not use a hyphen if the adjective follows the noun: 'the book was well read'.
- Do not use a hyphen following the prefixes re and pre, except where these are followed by another 'e': re-entry, pre-empt (but reopen and preconceive).

Note: English does not use an omission hyphen to avoid repeating part of the composite word. Incorrect: in- and external communications. Correct: internal and external communications.

### Quotation marks

Use double quotation marks when quoting a person, as in: "Any intelligent fool can make things bigger and more complex. It takes a touch of genius – and a lot of courage – to move in the opposite direction." Albert Einstein, 1879-1955. When the quotation is a full sentence, the full stop comes before the close quotes.

Use single quotation marks for:

- A quote within a quote, as in: "The chairman said, 'We will wait for a few more minutes,' and I was in total agreement."
- On-screen messages, such as instructions for system applications.
- Titles of seminars and conferences.

### Semicolons

The semicolon is used to mark a pause longer than a comma and shorter than a full stop. When in doubt, use a full stop.

### Square brackets

Use square brackets to insert clarifying words into a passage already being quoted. The words within the brackets are not part of the quoted text: The chairman said, "The rate increase [in Belgium] will affect the market."

nodes.title: 6. Inclusive writing
nodes.summary: This document outlines the principles and practices of inclusive writing, emphasizing the goal of reaching all readers and making them feel valued. It covers using plain language, avoiding jargon, and structuring text effectively. The guide stresses the importance of gender-neutral language, providing strategies for avoiding gendered pronouns and job titles, and suggests opting for wording with feminine connotations that fosters community. It also advocates for people-first language and the avoidance of unconscious bias and labeling. Finally, it recommends peer review for identifying blind spots and points to UN guidelines for further information.
nodes.text:
## 6. Inclusive writing

Reach everyone with your text

At ABN AMRO, we want to help people move forward with action-focused, personal and accessible texts. We don't want to exclude anyone, and language plays a key role. All it takes is one poorly chosen word or inadequate explanation to give a large target group the feeling the text isn't meant for them. But when you succeed in writing without unconscious bias or blind spots, every reader will feel valued, seen and taken seriously.

ABN·AMRO

Writing Guide • January 2026
Inclusive writing

### 6.1 Use plain language

Not everyone in our society finds reading easy. To make sure your message also gets across to readers who struggle, it's important to pay attention to things like jargon, length of sentences, structure and style. Check chapter 3, 'Wording your message' for more information.

### 6.2 Use gender-neutral language

Attention to gender-neutral, non-sexist language has been on the rise for decades. This has made us increasingly aware of words that can be experienced as biased, discriminatory or derogatory because they suggest that a certain gender is standard.

### Gender-neutral pronouns

The idea that a person is a man or a woman is deeply engrained in our language. This is clear from our personal pronouns: 'he', 'she', 'his' and 'her'. You should avoid these words. There are various ways to do this. Let's take the following sentence as an example: 'The bank employee should make sure his/her name badge is always visible.'

- Use a definite article rather than a personal pronoun
'The bank employee should make sure the name badge is always visible.'
- Address the reader directly
'As a bank employee, you should make sure your name badge is always visible.'
- Use the plural rather than the singular
'Bank employees should make sure their name badge is always visible.'
- Use they/them/their as a singular pronoun. This has become common practice in English
'The bank employee should make sure their name badge is always visible.'

### Gender-neutral job titles

A lot of job titles suggest that the job is specifically done by a man or a woman. Something else you see a lot is the word 'female' or 'woman' added to job titles when the writer apparently associates a certain job title more with men than with women. Whether they do this consciously or subconsciously, it's certainly wrong, so use a more general, gender-neutral alternative instead.

|  Gender-specific | Gender-neutral  |
| --- | --- |
|  Businesswoman | Businessperson  |
|  Saleswoman | Salesperson / Sales representative  |

Writing Guide • January 2026
Inclusive writing

### Words with a feminine connotation

Some words have a masculine or feminine connotation that tells you something about the associations many readers have with that word and how they will respond to your text. For instance, words that express something individualistic, competitive, ambitious or assertive are generally more appropriate for men. Research shows that women actually respond better to language that creates a sense of community, social and emotional words, dedication, support, compassion and understanding.

Women have a clear aversion to masculine texts, while men don't mind feminine or neutral language. In light of this, opt for more feminine wording where possible. That way, you'll reach the biggest audience.

|  Masculine | Feminine  |
| --- | --- |
|  Dominate | Excel  |
|  Analyse | Understand  |
|  Ambitious, decisive | Dedicated  |
|  Independent, individual | Together, cooperative  |
|  Dominant, superior | Accessible, polite, sensitive  |

### Use people-first language

You sometimes need to mention a specific group, if you want to say something about people who are deaf or blind, for instance. Always put the person first in such cases.

|  Not like this | Like this  |
| --- | --- |
|  The blind | Person with a vision/visual disability  |
|  The deaf | Person with a hearing disability  |
|  Foreigners/immigrants | People with an immigrant background  |

Writing Guide • January 2026
Inclusive writing

### Avoid unconscious bias

The main point of inclusive communication is keeping unconscious bias and blind spots out of your text, so you don't exclude anybody or make them feel 'different'. Be aware of your assumptions.

☐ Not like this
☑ Like this

|  Women in the world of finance earn much less. | Women in the world of finance earn much less than men.  |
| --- | --- |
|  People with an immigration background have to work harder to get a promotion. | People with an immigration background have to work harder than people born in the Netherlands to get a promotion.  |

In these examples, the first sentence implies a norm, i.e. 'men' or 'people born in the Netherlands'. The other group mentioned in the sentences is portrayed as different, as people who deviate from the norm in the author's view.

The same thing happens when you give people labels, so be sure to avoid these. In most cases, they aren't necessary to get your message across.

☐ Not like this
☑ Like this

|  working mother | mother  |
| --- | --- |
|  female director | director  |
|  manager with an immigrant background | manager  |

### Tip: have a colleague read your text

Being aware of your biases is easier said than done. There's a reason why they're called 'blind spots': often you are unaware that you have assumed a certain norm. So it's a good idea to have a colleague read through your text.

### More about inclusive language

Check the UN guidelines to find out more.
